title: Repairing a chipped chair leg with epoxy
subtitle: Build up a missing corner on a wooden chair leg with two-part epoxy putty
excerpt: How to rebuild a chipped corner of a wooden chair leg using two-part epoxy putty. Covers cleaning the chip, mixing the putty to the right consistency, shaping the replacement with a wet finger, and finishing to match the surrounding wood.

glossaryTerms.definition: A putty with two strands or balls of different colour that activates when kneaded together. Sets to a hard plastic that can be sanded, drilled, and finished like wood. Sold in tubes for furniture and trim repair.

content.text: A chipped corner on a chair leg catches every passing trouser hem until it is fixed. Two-part
epoxy putty
rebuilds the missing corner in 30 minutes. The cured putty sands and stains like the surrounding timber.
Step 1, clean the chip

Wipe loose splinters off the chip with a dry brush. If the chip is jagged, pare the broken edges back to clean wood with the chisel. Sand the inside of the chip with 120 grit so the epoxy has a key to grip.
Step 2, mix the epoxy

Cut a small piece of putty from the tube. Knead the two colours together with damp fingers until the colour is even. The putty has a working time of about 4 minutes from full mix before it starts to set; work briskly.
Step 3, press into the chip

Press the mixed putty firmly into the chip. Push it into the back of the cavity first, then build up to the surface. Overfill by 1 to 2 mm so there is material to sand back. Shape the surface roughly to the line of the chair leg with a damp fingertip.
Step 4, smooth with a damp finger

Wet a fingertip with clean water. Run it across the putty surface in long strokes to smooth out finger prints and feather the edges of the repair into the surrounding wood. The water stops the putty sticking to the finger. Re-wet often.
Step 5, allow to cure
Leave the repair for 60 minutes for a workable cure, or 24 hours for full cure. The full cure is needed before staining or varnishing; an under-cured epoxy soaks up stain unevenly.
Step 6, sand to shape

Sand the cured repair with 120 grit on the sanding block, then 180 grit, then 240 grit. Match the shape and the grain direction of the surrounding leg. The repair should sit flush with the timber on every side, with no raised edge or step.
Step 7, finish to match

On a painted chair leg, prime the repair area with a stain-blocking primer, then paint two coats of the matching colour. On a stained leg, apply the matching stain to a test patch first; cured epoxy can read darker or lighter than wood. Build the colour with two or three thin stain passes if needed, then seal with the matching varnish or oil.
Variations

On a chip where the missing corner is bigger than a fingernail, glue a sliver of matching wood into the cavity first as a core, then build up the surface with epoxy. The wood core takes most of the load and the epoxy fills the seam. On a turned leg with a visible grain pattern, a grain-painted finish over the cured epoxy reads truer than a single-colour blend.
Care

An epoxy repair on a chair leg lasts the life of the chair. The repair is harder than the wood around it; over decades, the surrounding wood may wear back so the repair sits slightly proud. A light sand and refinish brings it back level.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The epoxy sets sticky and never hardens
attrs.cause: The two colours were not fully mixed before pressing in.
attrs.fix: Cut the sticky epoxy out. Mix a fresh batch by kneading the two colours until the marbling is gone and only one even colour remains.

attrs.symptom: Stain colour reads wrong on the cured epoxy
attrs.cause: Cured epoxy absorbs stain differently from wood.
attrs.fix: Lightly sand the repair and apply a tinted wax or grain-painted layer in the matching shade rather than a wood stain. The wax sits on the surface and matches by eye.

attrs.symptom: Repair pops off the chair leg under pressure
attrs.cause: Inside of the chip was not keyed, or the cured epoxy reached the surrounding paint or varnish rather than bare wood.
attrs.fix: Remove the loose repair. Sand back to bare wood across the chip area, key with 120 grit, and reapply fresh epoxy.
